_vars.raw_problem: We need you to design a syntactic parser that can accurately analyze English sentences for our linguistic research project. The parser should identify and label the different syntactic structures present in the sentences. While the primary focus is on declarative sentences, it should also handle questions and commands effectively. Your output should be a software prototype that demonstrates the parsing capabilities with sample sentences. Consider incorporating contemporary linguistic theories, but keep in mind the practical constraints of computational efficiency. No specific sentence dataset is provided, so you'll need to determine suitable examples. Additionally, decide on the evaluation criteria to assess the parser's performance, but ensure it aligns with industry standards. Please document your design choices and assumptions in a brief report accompanying the software.
_vars.student_framing: [{"section_type": "Problem Definition", "content": "We need to create a program that can take English sentences and break them down into parts to show how they are structured. This means finding things like subjects, verbs, and objects."}, {"section_type": "Scope", "content": "The program should mainly focus on sentences that make statements (declarative), but it should also be able to understand questions and commands. We will look at simple sentences first."}, {"section_type": "Requirements", "content": "The parser needs to label different parts of the sentences correctly. We should also think about how fast it runs, so it doesn't take too long to analyze sentences."}, {"section_type": "Dataset Selection", "content": "We should find some example sentences to test our parser. These can be made up or from books. We need a mix of declarative sentences, questions, and commands."}, {"section_type": "Evaluation Criteria", "content": "We can check how well our parser works by looking at how many parts it gets right compared to the actual sentence structure. We should also see if it runs quickly enough."}, {"section_type": "Documentation", "content": "We need to write a short report that explains how we built the parser, what choices we made, and why we made them. This will help others understand our work."}]

criteria.name: Identification of Core Syntactic Structures
criteria.description: Ability to identify and define the main syntactic structures relevant to the problem.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Clearly identifies relevant syntactic structures such as noun phrases, verb phrases, and clauses.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to identify or incorrectly labels basic syntactic structures.

criteria.name: Clarification of Ambiguities
criteria.description: Ability to recognize and clarify syntactic ambiguities in the problem statement.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Identifies potential ambiguities and provides clear distinctions between possible interpretations.
criteria.poor_indicator: Overlooks ambiguities or provides vague clarifications.

criteria.name: Specification of Parsing Goals
criteria.description: Ability to translate the problem into specific parsing tasks and objectives.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Clearly defines the parsing goals with a focus on extracting syntactic relationships.
criteria.poor_indicator: Provides unclear or overly broad parsing goals without focus.

typical_errors: Confusing syntactic categories with semantic roles.

best_framing.base: We need you to design a syntactic parser that can accurately analyze English sentences for our linguistic research project. The parser should identify and label the different syntactic structures present in the sentences. While the primary focus is on declarative sentences, it should also handle questions and commands effectively. Your output should be a software prototype that demonstrates the parsing capabilities with sample sentences. Consider incorporating contemporary linguistic theories, but keep in mind the practical constraints of computational efficiency. No specific sentence dataset is provided, so you'll need to determine suitable examples. Additionally, decide on the evaluation criteria to assess the parser's performance, but ensure it aligns with industry standards. Please document your design choices and assumptions in a brief report accompanying the software.

entries.title: Contemporary Linguistic Theories
entries.description: Assume that contemporary linguistic theories such as Chomsky's Minimalist Program and Dependency Grammar are relevant for this task. These theories provide a framework for understanding syntactic structures and can guide the parser's design.
entries.addresses_gap: What contemporary linguistic theories to incorporate.

entries.title: Evaluation Criteria
entries.description: Assume that the evaluation criteria will include precision, recall, and F1 score, as these are standard metrics in syntactic parsing tasks. Additionally, computational efficiency should be considered, measured by parsing time per sentence.
entries.addresses_gap: What the evaluation criteria should be.

entries.title: Computational Efficiency
entries.description: The parser must operate efficiently, ideally processing sentences in linear or near-linear time complexity to ensure scalability.

entries.title: Scope of Sentence Types
entries.description: Clarify that the parser should handle declarative sentences as the primary focus, but must also be capable of parsing interrogative and imperative sentences effectively.
entries.addresses_gap: Ambiguity in the types of sentences to be parsed.

entries.title: Syntactic Structure Identification
entries.description: Success is measured by the parser's ability to accurately identify and label core syntactic structures such as noun phrases, verb phrases, prepositional phrases, and clauses.
entries.addresses_gap: What specific syntactic structures need to be identified.

entries.title: Handling of Sentence Variability
entries.description: The parser should demonstrate robust performance across a variety of sentence types and complexities, including compound and complex sentences.

entries.title: Inclusion of Sample Sentences
entries.description: The scope includes selecting a diverse set of sample sentences that reflect various syntactic structures and sentence types. This selection should include both simple and complex sentences to test the parser's capabilities.
entries.addresses_gap: No specific sentence dataset is provided.

best_framing.coverage_notes: This framing addresses all rubric criteria by clearly defining syntactic structures, clarifying ambiguities, and specifying parsing goals. It ensures that the parser's design is aligned with contemporary linguistic theories and industry standards for evaluation.

criticism: The student's framing lacks specificity in identifying core syntactic structures. The response mentions 'subjects, verbs, and objects' but does not address more complex structures like noun phrases, verb phrases, or clauses, which are crucial for a syntactic parser. Additionally, the student does not clarify any potential ambiguities in the problem statement, such as how to handle compound or complex sentences. The parsing goals are somewhat vague, focusing on speed and correctness without detailing specific syntactic relationships to be extracted.
better_alternative: A stronger framing would explicitly identify and define the core syntactic structures that the parser needs to recognize, such as noun phrases, verb phrases, prepositional phrases, and clauses. It would also address potential ambiguities by specifying how the parser should handle different sentence types, including compound and complex sentences. Additionally, the parsing goals should be clearly articulated, focusing on extracting specific syntactic relationships and ensuring the parser's ability to handle sentence variability effectively.

per_criterion_scores.internal_note: The student only mentioned basic elements like subjects, verbs, and objects, missing more complex structures.
No effort was made to clarify potential ambiguities in sentence types or structures.
The goals are somewhat defined but lack specificity regarding syntactic relationships and sentence variability.

strengths: The student correctly identified the need to focus on declarative, interrogative, and imperative sentences.
The student acknowledged the importance of computational efficiency.
